content.text: Red, green, refactor — those three words describe a discipline that reverses the conventional coding workflow: instead of writing code first and testing it later, Test-Driven Development (TDD) demands that every production change begins with a failing automated test that specifies the desired behaviour. The discipline is decades old but has never been more relevant than in agentic AI systems, where LLM calls, tool invocations, and multi-step reasoning chains create complex, non-deterministic pipelines that are notoriously difficult to debug after the fact.
Test-Driven Development is a software development process in which an engineer writes an automated test
writing the implementation code that will satisfy it. The test encodes the intended contract of a function, class, or system component. The workflow follows a tight three-phase micro-cycle:
— write a test that expresses a new requirement; run it; confirm it fails (the feature does not yet exist).
— write the minimum production code necessary to make the failing test pass; no more, no less.
— improve the implementation's structure, clarity, and efficiency while keeping the full test suite green.

Each micro-cycle typically completes in two to fifteen minutes. Hundreds of such cycles compound into a fully tested, well-structured codebase with a granular commit history that maps directly to verified requirements. TDD is not a testing strategy in isolation — it is a design methodology, because writing a test first forces the engineer to reason about the public interface, the expected inputs, and the observable outputs before touching implementation details.

In agentic AI contexts, TDD applies at multiple granularities: individual pure functions (prompt builders, response parsers, tool selectors), agent orchestration logic, retry-and-fallback decorators, memory-retrieval helpers, and safety guardrails. The non-determinism of LLM responses does not block TDD — it is instead managed by injecting controllable stubs that return predictable canned outputs, leaving deterministic application logic as the direct subject of each test.

Agentic AI pipelines are uniquely difficult to debug post-hoc. A silent failure deep in a tool-routing function can cause an agent to pick the wrong action hundreds of times before an operator notices an outcome anomaly. By then, the root cause may be obscured by log volume and model-version drift. TDD surfaces such failures at the moment of introduction — a broken implementation cannot be committed without first witnessing the test that catches it.
The discipline also directly addresses two chronic pains in AI development:

— when designing a new prompt-builder or response-parser, writing the test first obliges an explicit decision about what the function should accept and return, even before deciding how it works internally. This eliminates vague, half-specified interfaces that later become change magnets.

— agentic systems are frequently updated with new model versions, prompt tweaks, and tool-schema changes. Without tests, each update is a leap of faith. With TDD's accumulated suite, every change is validated in seconds across hundreds of edge cases that the team documented while originally implementing the features.

Beyond safety nets, TDD acts as living documentation. A well-named test tells the next engineer exactly what a component is supposed to do, what inputs are expected, and which edge cases have been considered — far more precisely than any prose comment or README.
— bugs are caught at the point of introduction, when context is fresh and the fix is cheap.
— to write a passing test, the implementation must be injectable and loosely coupled, naturally discouraging tight coupling to live LLM clients or databases.
— every feature accumulated by the codebase is exercised on every run; new changes cannot silently break old functionality.
— structural improvements can be made aggressively because the test suite acts as an instant oracle.
— tests express requirements in a machine-verifiable form; they can be shared with stakeholders as acceptance criteria before a line of production code is written.
— a failing test names the broken unit immediately, versus hours of log-diving in an untested system.
— TDD's need for isolation pushes code toward smaller, single-responsibility units that are easier to compose and reason about.
— writing tests before implementation increases the time to produce the first working line of code. Teams under extreme schedule pressure may feel this acutely in the short term.
— as production code evolves, tests must be updated. Poorly written tests that test implementation details rather than behaviour become brittle and expensive to maintain.
— raw LLM responses are variable; TDD must be applied to the
around LLM calls, not the model itself, which requires discipline to keep mocks realistic.
— teams new to TDD sometimes write tests for private implementation details, accumulating a fragile suite that breaks on every internal refactor.
— developers unfamiliar with mocking, test doubles, and the red-green-refactor rhythm require a period of adjustment before productivity recovers.
— a passing suite that does not cover critical paths creates an illusion of safety. Coverage metrics are a floor, not a ceiling.

Each TDD iteration begins when the engineer picks the next smallest piece of behaviour to add or fix. This requirement is encoded as a test: the test imports the function or class under development, invokes it with specific inputs, and asserts on outputs or side effects. Running the suite at this point shows the new test failing (red), confirming the test actually detects absence of the feature.

The engineer then writes exactly enough production code to satisfy the assertion — not a general solution, not the full feature, just enough. Premature generalisation at this stage is a common error. Running the suite again must show every test green. If other tests regressed, the implementation is wrong and must be corrected before proceeding.

Refactoring follows: the code is improved structurally — duplication is removed, names are clarified, abstractions are extracted — while confirming the suite stays green after every incremental change. When refactoring is complete, the next cycle begins.

Although TDD naturally produces high coverage, teams track it formally using metrics such as line coverage, branch coverage, and mutation score. Branch coverage is especially important for agentic code because decision points (route-to-tool A or tool B, retry or abort, inject memory or skip) represent the business logic. A coverage target of
(90 % branch coverage) is a common floor for production agentic components.

attrs.latex: ext{mutation score} =  rac{|	ext{killed mutants}|}{|	ext{total mutants}|}

content.text: Mutation testing — where the test suite is run against automatically broken variants of the production code — provides a deeper signal than raw line coverage. A suite with 95 % line coverage but 40 % mutation score is revealing that many tests do not actually check outputs.
The diagram below shows the red-green-refactor cycle centred on a typical agentic AI component, with the test harness as the driver and the LLM and tools abstracted behind controllable fakes.
is the unit under test: a prompt builder, response parser, tool router, memory interface, or safety hook. The

(pytest / unittest) owns the test runner, the mock/stub injection, and the assertion layer. The real LLM client and real external tools are never called during unit tests — they are replaced by deterministic doubles that return pre-configured values. Integration tests, run in a separate CI stage, exercise the real LLM against a sandboxed environment with budget constraints.
Building new agentic components from scratch, especially ones with well-defined input/output contracts (parsers, routers, validators).
Fixing a bug: write a failing test that reproduces the bug, then fix the implementation until the test passes — guarantees the bug cannot silently return.
Implementing safety-critical logic: guardrails, PII scrubbers, rate limiters — components where correctness is non-negotiable.
Refactoring legacy pipeline code that currently lacks tests: add tests for the existing behaviour first, then refactor under their protection.
Implementing retry, back-off, and circuit-breaker decorators where timing behaviour can be controlled with a fake clock.
Exploratory prototyping where the interface is still unknown — write exploratory code first, then extract a tested module once the shape is clear.
Direct prompt engineering experiments on live models — these require empirical evaluation loops rather than unit assertions.
Infrastructure-level concerns (container orchestration, network config) where integration or smoke tests are more appropriate than unit tests.
— identify the smallest named piece of behaviour to add (e.g., 'parser correctly extracts tool-call block from LLM response').
— create a test function that sets up inputs, calls the unit, and asserts the expected output using
— execute the test suite; the new test must fail. If it passes immediately, either the code already exists or the test is wrong.
— implement just enough to satisfy the assertion; resist adding unrelated logic.
— all tests must pass. If other tests break, fix the implementation, not the tests.
— rename variables, extract helpers, remove duplication. Re-run after every change.
— a small, atomic commit with a descriptive message tying the test to the requirement.
— pick the next requirement and begin the next micro-cycle.
A prompt builder assembles the system prompt from runtime context. The test is written first, specifying what the function must include.

# test_prompt_builder.py
import pytest
from agent.prompt_builder import build_system_prompt

def test_includes_tool_list():
    tools = ["search_web", "read_file"]
    result = build_system_prompt(tools=tools, locale="en")
    assert "search_web" in result
    assert "read_file" in result

def test_locale_injected():
    result = build_system_prompt(tools=[], locale="fr")
    assert "fr" in result

def test_empty_tools_no_crash():
    result = build_system_prompt(tools=[], locale="en")
    assert isinstance(result, str)
    assert len(result) > 0
Running these tests immediately fails because
does not exist. The implementation is then written to satisfy exactly these assertions.
An LLM response parser extracts tool-call blocks from raw text. The tests cover the happy path, multiple calls, and malformed input.

# test_response_parser.py
from agent.response_parser import parse_tool_calls

VALID = """
Some reasoning text.
<tool_call>{"name": "search_web", "args": {"q": "TDD"}}</tool_call>
More text.
<tool_call>{"name": "read_file", "args": {"path": "/tmp/x.txt"}}</tool_call>
"""

MALFORMED = """
<tool_call>{bad json}</tool_call>
"""

def test_parses_two_calls():
    calls = parse_tool_calls(VALID)
    assert len(calls) == 2
    assert calls[0]["name"] == "search_web"
    assert calls[1]["name"] == "read_file"

def test_skips_malformed_json():
    calls = parse_tool_calls(MALFORMED)
    assert calls == []

def test_no_calls_returns_empty():
    assert parse_tool_calls("Just regular text.") == []
Retry decorator for an LLM client

Production agentic systems must handle transient API errors (rate limits, network timeouts). A retry decorator with exponential back-off is safety-critical: incorrect logic can cascade into infinite loops or fail to retry recoverable errors. TDD is ideal because timing is deterministic when a fake clock replaces
Testing implementation details instead of behaviour

Asserting on internal variable names or private method calls means the test breaks every time the implementation is refactored, even if the behaviour is identical. Tests should assert on observable outputs — return values, raised exceptions, calls to collaborator interfaces — not on how the implementation achieves them.
Writing the test after the code

Writing the test retrospectively removes TDD's design pressure. When implementation already exists, there is a strong cognitive bias to write tests that simply confirm what the code does rather than what it
do. This produces tests that pass for the wrong reasons and miss genuine edge cases.

A test function that exercises ten different scenarios in sequence is fragile: a failure in step three obscures whether steps four through ten would also fail. Each test function should verify exactly one behaviour; the test name documents the scenario. Short, focused tests produce failure messages that immediately identify the broken requirement.
Not running the test first to confirm it fails

Skipping the 'run red' step means the engineer never confirms the test is actually testing anything. A test that passes before the implementation exists is either testing the wrong thing or is checking a condition that is trivially true. The red step is a verification of the test itself, not just a ritual.

Mocking every dependency in a unit test risks creating a test that verifies the mock, not the production code. Mock only what is non-deterministic, slow, or has unacceptable side effects (live LLM calls, network I/O, file writes). Pure logic that can run in memory should not be mocked.
— group related assertions, but keep each test focused on a single behaviour so failures are immediately interpretable.
— structure every test in three clearly separated phases: set up the context, invoke the unit, assert on the result.
Prefer real objects over mocks where cheap
— if a helper class is fast and side-effect-free, use it directly rather than mocking it; this keeps tests closer to reality.
— if a cycle takes more than 20 minutes, the scope of the requirement being implemented is too large. Split it.
Run the full suite on every commit
— configure CI to reject any commit that breaks the suite. Never let a red suite sit overnight.
— test quality deserves the same scrutiny as production code quality. Weak tests generate a false sense of security.
Track mutation score, not just line coverage
— coverage can be 100 % while mutation score is 40 %, which means many tests are not actually catching errors.
A well-maintained TDD suite runs in seconds locally and under two minutes in CI. As the codebase grows, naive approaches hit scale problems:
— if tests share mutable global state, they must be run serially. Stateless tests can be parallelised with
, reducing suite time proportionally to available CPU cores.
— repeated construction of large objects (large prompt templates, deep agent graphs) in every test slows the suite. Use pytest fixtures with
— a test that calls a real database or real LLM API takes seconds per test. Replacing these with in-memory fakes (sqlite in-memory, a stub LLM client) brings each test back to milliseconds.

The general target is: unit tests under 10 ms each; the full unit suite under 30 s; integration tests (real services, sandboxed) under 5 min; end-to-end tests (real model, full pipeline) under 15 min. Tests exceeding these thresholds become CI bottlenecks and are often disabled or skipped — defeating the entire point of TDD.
is the number of tests and
The de-facto Python testing framework for agentic AI work. Fixtures, parametrisation, plugins (
for spy/stub helpers) make it well-suited to the varied testing needs of agentic pipelines.

# install testing dependencies
pip install pytest pytest-asyncio pytest-mock pytest-xdist coverage

# run with coverage report
coverage run -m pytest tests/unit/ -v
coverage report --fail-under=90

# run tests in parallel (4 workers)
pytest tests/unit/ -n 4
— sufficient for stubbing LLM clients, tool functions, and HTTP sessions without additional dependencies.

Hypothesis generates thousands of semi-random inputs from a developer-specified strategy, complementing TDD's example-based tests. For functions like token counters, text splitters, and JSON validators, property-based tests catch edge cases that no human would enumerate.

# property_test_parser.py
from hypothesis import given, strategies as st
from agent.response_parser import parse_tool_calls

@given(st.text())
def test_parser_never_raises(text):
    """Parser must handle arbitrary string input without crashing."""
    result = parse_tool_calls(text)
    assert isinstance(result, list)
TDD occupies a specific point in a spectrum of testing approaches. The table below compares the most common alternatives on dimensions relevant to agentic AI development.
Tests often miss edge cases; weaker design pressure
Low for unit logic; useful for UX
Validates that tests catch real errors
Slow to run; not a first-line tool

Security-critical agentic behaviours — PII scrubbing, prompt-injection detection, access-control enforcement — must have explicit test coverage. Writing the tests first forces the engineer to enumerate attack scenarios before building the defence, producing a test suite that serves as a living security specification.
— write tests that pass adversarial strings ('ignore all previous instructions') and assert that the sanitiser neutralises them before they reach the model.
— parameterise tests over known PII patterns (email, SSN, phone) and assert redacted output.
— test that tool-routing logic rejects tool calls that require elevated permissions for the current session.

Every production incident that is traced back to a regression in application logic represents a test that was not written when the feature was first built. A TDD-disciplined team treats each incident debrief as a testing gap: the first remediation action is to write a test that would have caught the defect, committed to the repository as a permanent guard against recurrence.

In agentic systems with long-running memory stores, tests should also validate invariants over time: that memory entries are correctly evicted, that token budgets are respected across multi-turn sessions, and that tool-use history is not leaked between user sessions.
Think of TDD as writing the product acceptance criteria

before building the product. A test is a specification written in the same language as the implementation — precise, executable, and automatically verified. The red phase proves the spec does not yet pass. The green phase proves the implementation satisfies it. The refactor phase proves the implementation can be improved without violating the spec.

An analogy from engineering: before building a bridge, the structural requirements are written down (load capacity, deflection limits, safety factors). TDD does the same for software: before writing a function, the requirements are written down as assertions. If the function later changes, the tests immediately tell the team whether the change respected or violated the original requirements.
Another useful frame: TDD is a

. Every new test adds a line item to an auditable ledger of verified behaviours. Every time the suite runs green, the ledger is reconfirmed. A green suite does not prove the system has no bugs, but it does prove that every behaviour that has ever been specified in a test is still working. The depth of the ledger — the breadth and quality of its test cases — determines how much confidence that green represents.
TDD reverses the conventional workflow: the test is the specification, written before the implementation, and the failing test (red) is the first verifiable proof that the specification is real.
The red-green-refactor micro-cycle keeps changes small, focused, and always verifiable — each iteration completes in minutes, not hours.
In agentic AI systems, TDD applies to the application-layer logic around LLM calls (parsers, routers, builders, retry logic), not to the non-deterministic model output itself.
Inject controllable stubs in place of live LLM clients and external tools during unit tests; reserve real-service calls for a separate, budget-bounded integration stage.
Test names are documentation: a suite of well-named tests communicates requirements more precisely than any prose spec and is guaranteed to be kept up to date.
Measure test quality with mutation score, not just line coverage — a suite can have 100 % coverage while failing to catch obvious bugs.
Security-critical behaviours (prompt-injection guards, PII scrubbers, access controls) gain the most from TDD because the tests enumerate attack scenarios before the defence is built.
A failing test after a production incident is the first line of remediation: committing it to the repository makes recurrence of that specific defect impossible to miss.
